g.data-narration: This lesson covers alarm and pause functions in Unix and Linux systems, essential for time-based operations and process synchronization in system programming applications.
g.text: Alarm and Pause Functions in Unix/Linux

g.data-narration: The alarm function schedules a SIGALRM signal after specified seconds. Pause suspends process until a signal arrives, enabling wait mechanisms in Unix systems.
g.text: Introduction
• alarm(seconds): Schedules SIGALRM signal after specified seconds
• pause(): Suspends process until a signal is delivered
• Used for timeouts, watchdog timers, periodic tasks in system programs

g.data-narration: The alarm function prototype is unsigned int alarm(unsigned int seconds). It returns seconds remaining from previous alarm, or zero if no previous alarm was set.
g.text: The alarm() Function
Prototype: unsigned int alarm(unsigned int seconds);
• Schedules SIGALRM (signal 14) after 'seconds' elapsed
• alarm(0) cancels any pending alarm
• Returns: seconds remaining from previous alarm, or 0 if none

g.data-narration: The pause function suspends execution until a signal arrives and is handled. Returns -1 with errno EINTR only when a signal handler is invoked by the system.
g.text: The pause() Function
Prototype: int pause(void);
• Suspends calling process until any signal is delivered
• Returns only when signal handler invoked (returns -1, errno=EINTR)
• Default SIGALRM action terminates process - must install handler!

g.data-narration: Always set alarm BEFORE pause to avoid race conditions in your code. If the signal arrives before pause is called, the process will block indefinitely on pause.
g.text: Race Condition Warning
✓ CORRECT ORDER:
alarm(5); pause();
✗ WRONG ORDER:
pause(); alarm(5); (race!)
Setting alarm first ensures signal delivery even if it fires just before pause() call

g.data-narration: Use sigaction instead of signal for robust and portable signal handling. Signal handlers must only use async-signal-safe functions like write, never printf for output.
g.text: Signal Handling Best Practices
• Use sigaction() instead of signal() for portable robust handling
• Use volatile sig_atomic_t for flags shared with handlers
• Handlers: only use async-signal-safe functions (write, _exit), NOT printf
• Alarms persist across exec() but NOT across fork()

g.data-narration: This example implements timeout read functionality: set SIGALRM handler, call alarm(5), attempt read operation, cancel alarm on success. Remember: alarm must be called before the operation.
g.text: Example: Timeout Read Implementation
signal(SIGALRM, handler); alarm(5); n = read(fd, buf, size); alarm(0);
Key: alarm(0) cancels alarm | setitimer() for repeating timers | Default SIGALRM terminates process
